segments.s727-g97: بدو گفت is a light prepositional phrase + verb: بدو (to him) precedes گفت (said) and marks the indirect object. شویی here is an archaic/poetic second‑person predicate form (equivalent to “thou art / you are” or ‘you who are’); کز introduces a relative clause (’who/that’) and ایران بود is the relatively simple past copula clause ‘was from Iran.’ Word order is typical older Persian: indirect object (بدو) + verb (گفت) + predicate (شویی) + relative phrase (کز ... بود).
segments.s727-g98: ازو functions as an agent/source phrase ‘from him / by him’; تخمه ما is the possessed noun phrase ‘our seed / our stock’ with ما (our). نه is the negator placed before the adjective ویران, and بود is the past copula, so نه ویران بود = ‘was not ruined.’ The whole segment means ‘our line was not ruined by him.’
segments.s727-g99: This is an ergative/agentive‑style clause: یلان (heroes) is the plural subject/agent, سینه او را is the definite object (‘his chest’) marked by را, and بگستهم داد is a compound verb where ب-گستهم (to broaden/spread) plus داد (gave) yields ‘gave breadth/expanded.’ The order Subject + Object + Verb (with object marked by را) is normal for older Persian.
segments.s727-g100: دلاور گوی بوده (here condensed as دلاور گوی بود) is a nominal predicate: دلاور describes the quality ‘valiant/brave’; گوی in this poetic context functions as a verbal/attributive element meaning ‘called/said’ (archaic/poetic usage), and بود is the past copula ‘was.’ فرخ نژاد is a two‑word epithet meaning ‘of fortunate (فرخ) lineage (نژاد).’ The clause thus states he was called brave and of a fortunate lineage.
segments.s727-g101: همی‌داشتش is a single verbal form meaning ‘he kept/held/borne him’ (همی‑ is continuous/aspectual in older Persian, داشتش = had/held him). چون introduces a simile ‘like/as,’ followed by یکی تازه سیب ‘a single/fresh apple.’ The phrase reads ‘he held him like a fresh apple.’
segments.s727-g102: که introduces a relative clause modifying the apple: اندر = in/on, بلندی = height (here ‘on high’), ندیدی is the negative past ‘you have not seen,’ and نشیب means ‘descent/fall.’ The clause literally says ‘which, in its height, you have not seen descend’ — i.e. ‘you have never seen it fall from on high.’
segments.s727-g103: سپاهی is ‘a soldier/warrior’ (here a nominal subject). که introduces a relative clause: از نزد خسرو = ‘from/by the presence of Khosrow’ (نزد = in the presence of), and شدی is a past form meaning ‘became / had been’ (here indicating he belonged to or had served at Khosrow’s court). The whole segment: ‘a warrior who had been at Khosrow’s side.’
segments.s727-g104: برو is an adverbial/particle often glossed ‘then/so’ or literally ‘go’ in older usage; روزگار کهن نو شدی is a predicate meaning ‘the old time/age (روزگار کهن) became new’ with شدی as the past of شدن (‘became’). The clause states that the ancient times were renewed.
segments.s727-g9: بگفت is the past narrative 'he said'. آنچ (classical form of آن‌چه) means 'that which' or 'this/that thing' and introduces what was said. فرمان پرویز بود is a copular clause 'was Parviz's command' (فرمان + اسم + بود), with بود as the past copula 'was'.
segments.s727-g10: که introduces a reported clause 'that'. شاه جوان بود is 'the shah was young' (noun + adjective after it, with بود as past copula). و links the two predications, and خونریز بود repeats the copula to state 'was bloodthirsty' — the repeated بود is a classical/poetic feature for emphasis.
segments.s727-g11: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. گستهم here is the name Gostaham (proper name) and so does not match the literal dictionary sense given; the verb بشنید is past 'he heard'. لشکر براند is a verb phrase 'drove the army away' (لشکر = object/affected group, براند = drove off/put to flight). The subject (Gostaham) is understood to govern both verbs.
segments.s727-g12: This is a compressed poetic word order: پراگنده لشکر 'the scattered army' (adjective before noun here), همه 'all/the whole', and باز خواند 'called back' where باز is the adverb 'back/again' and خواند is the past 'he called/summoned'. The object marker را is omitted in classical style; the meaning is 'he called back the entire scattered host.'
segments.s727-g13: چنین تا functions as 'thus until' — a narrative connective marking motion up to a point. به شهر بزرگان رسید is 'he reached the city of the great (nobles)' with به marking direction/goal and رسید as the past 'arrived'. Note the noun + plural epithet بزرگان 'of the great ones'.
segments.s727-g14: ز here is the poetic form of 'from/through'. 'ز ساری و آمل به گرگان رسید' gives source/route then goal: from Sari and Amol he reached Gorgan. Sاری in the senses list is not given as the place-name sense here (so treated as a proper place name), and رسید is the past 'arrived'.
segments.s727-g15: شنید is past 'he heard' or 'learned'. آنک is the classical relative/conjunction 'that' (آن‌که). شد here means 'became' (a change of state), and شاه ایران درشت places the predicate adjective درشت 'harsh/cruel' after the nominal phrase; word order is inverted for poetic effect (شد precedes the subject).
segments.s727-g16: برادرش را is the definite object 'his brother' with the possessive ـش and the object marker را. او is the subject pronoun 'he' (here referring back to the shah). به مستی is an adverbial phrase 'in drunkenness', and بکشت is an archaic past-form meaning 'killed' (classical narrative past with the b- prefix + کشت). The clause states that he, while drunk, killed his brother; the order places the object before the subject/verb for emphasis.
segments.s727-g105: هر آنگه که is an archaic/poetic equivalent of modern Persian هر آن‌گاه که, meaning 'whenever' or 'whenever it was that'. دیدی is the past tense, 2nd-person singular ('you saw') — Ferdowsi frequently addresses a second person this way. شکست سپاه is a compressed nominal phrase meaning 'the army's rout/defeat'; the ezafe (شکستِ سپاه) is omitted in poetic diction, so شکست (a noun) stands before سپاه to indicate the defeat belonging to the army.
segments.s727-g106: کمان را is the definite object with the object marker را after کمان ('the bow'). بر افراشتی is 'you raised/lifted up' — افراشتی is past 2nd-person singular of افراشتن and بر gives the sense of upward/onward motion. تا به ماه is a hyperbolic adverbial phrase 'as far as/to the moon' (تا = 'as far as/until', به = 'to/toward'), completing the image of raising the bow up toward the moon.
segments.s727-g1: 'وزان پس' is a temporal/continuative phrase placed before the rest: 'from that time/after that'. Adverbial elements come early in Persian; then the directional phrase 'بسوی خراسان' ('toward Khorasan') precedes the subject 'کسی' ('someone').
segments.s727-g2: This contains two coordinated verb phrases. 'گسی کرد' is a past action ('کرد' = did/acted') with 'گسی' as an indefinite actor ('a certain person/someone'); 'و' links the second clause 'اندرز دادش بسی' where 'اندرز داد' is past tense 'gave advice' and the enclitic '-ش' on 'دادش' marks 'to him'. 'بسی' is an adverbial intensifier 'much/a great deal'.
segments.s727-g3: 'بدو گفت' = 'he said to him' with 'بدو' = 'to him' (dative) followed by the verb 'گفت' (past 'said'); Persian often places the indirect object before the verb. 'با کس مجنبان زبان' is an imperative prohibition: 'مجنبان' is a negative/imparative form meaning 'do not move' and 'با کس' ('with anyone') qualifies 'زبان' (tongue/speech) — i.e. 'do not speak to anyone' or 'do not open your mouth to anyone.'
segments.s727-g4: 'از ایدر برو' is an imperative: 'go away/from here' where 'از' marks source and 'ایدر' = 'here'. 'تا' marks the extent/goal 'as far as', and 'در' here is concrete 'door/gate' (the gate of the marzban), so 'تا در مرزبان' = 'as far as the marzban's gate' or more loosely 'to the marzban.'
segments.s727-g5: 'به گستهم گو' is an imperative: 'tell/ say to Gostaham' — 'به' marks the indirect object and 'گو' is the imperative 'say/tell'. 'ایچ گونه مپا' is a prohibition: 'ایچ گونه' = 'of such a kind / in no way' and 'مپا' is an archaic negative imperative meaning 'do not do', so the whole phrase means 'do not do such a thing.'
segments.s727-g6: 'چو' here is temporal 'when'. 'این نامه من' is a genitive phrase 'this letter of mine' (μονdern Persian 'my letter'); 'بخوانی' is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive ('you read' / 'when you read') used after a temporal clause, and 'بیا' is the imperative 'come' addressing the addressee.
segments.s727-g7: Temporal clause: 'فرستاده' (the envoy/messenger) is the subject; 'چون' introduces the temporal clause 'when'; 'در خراسان' is the locative phrase 'in Khorasan'; and 'رسید' is the past tense verb 'arrived/reached.' Word order places the location before the verb.
segments.s727-g8: 'به درگاه' = 'to the threshold / to the court' with 'به' marking motion toward. 'مرد تن آسان' is a nominal phrase: 'مرد' (man) qualified by the compound adjective 'تن آسان' ('easy/light of body' → gentle or mild-mannered). The final 'رسید' is a second past verb 'arrived/encountered' referring to the envoy reaching that place/person; repetition of رسید emphasizes sequence.
segments.s727-g17: چو (written here as چوبشنید) is the archaic poetic 'when' + past verb شنید 'he heard'. دستش is the noun 'hand' with the enclitic possessive -ش 'his'. به دندان بکند is a verblike phrase literally 'put/take (it) to the teeth' — here idiomatically 'bit his hand'; بکند is the classical poetic present/subjunctive-form used as an immediate action following چو (common in epic narrative) and functions as the main verb of this clause.
segments.s727-g18: فرود آمد is a compound verbal phrase 'alighted/sprang down' (فرود 'descent' + آمد 'came'), functioning as the past action. از پشت is the prepositional phrase 'from the back (of...)' and is followed by the two nouns اسپ سمند in apposition (both mean 'horse/steed', سمند giving the nuance 'warhorse'). Word order places the source phrase از پشت before the vehicle (اسپ سمند).
segments.s727-g19: همه جامه 'the whole garment' (همه modifying جامه). پهلوی marks the location 'at the side/flank' and چاک is used as verb here with کرد 'made/did' (چاک کردن) meaning 'to rend/tore' — literal order: 'he made a tear at the side of his whole garment.'
segments.s727-g20: خروشان is an adverbial participle/adverb 'roaring, tumultuously'. به سر بر همی‌ریخت خاک is an object phrase with serial verbs: به سر 'upon (his) head', بر همی‌ریخت (همی‌ریخت = past continuous 'was scattering/was throwing') and خاک 'dust' as the thing scattered — i.e. 'he was scattering dust upon his head' (a lamenting gesture).
segments.s727-g21: بدانست is simple past 'he knew/realized'. کو را here functions as a free interrogative/adverbial 'where' (with را marking the following phrase as the locus/object of knowledge); جهاندار شاه is an epithet + noun 'the sovereign king' in apposition and is the subject that will act in the following clause. The clause is elliptically continued by the next segment.
segments.s727-g22: به کین پدر is a causal/agentive prepositional phrase 'out of/for (the) vengeance of his father'. کرد خواهد تباه is an inverted periphrastic future/result construction: خواهید/خواهد (will) + کردن + adjective/noun تباه (ruined) — here meaning 'would make ruined' or 'would lay waste'; the sequence places کرد before خواهد in poetic word order but conveys future intention/result.
segments.s727-g23: خروشان is again adverbial 'roaring/tumultuously'. ازان جایگه is a locative prepositional phrase 'from that place' (ازان = از + ان 'from that'), and بازگشت is the past verbal 'he returned' (verb placed at the end as usual in Persian).
segments.s727-g24: تو گفتی که introduces reported speech 'you said that...'. In modern reading گفتی is 2nd-person past 'you said' and که is the complementizer 'that'. با باد انباز گشت is a simile/description 'he went/turned with the wind' — باد here 'wind' and انباز an archaic/uncertain adverbial (literally obscure, used adverbially), with گشت 'went/turned' as the verb of the reported clause.
segments.s727-g81: زبان تیز با گردیه بر گشاد is a compact poetic clause: زبان (here ‘speech/utterance’) is modified by تیز (‘sharp’) and accompanied by با + گردیه (‘with gathered/collected words’). بر گشاد is a verbal phrase meaning ‘opened (up) upon’ — the preposition بر + participial/adjectival گشاد (‘open/gaping’) describing the action of opening speech. Word order reflects a piled modifier style common in Persian poetry (modifier before core verb phrase).
segments.s727-g82: همی‌کرد is an iterative/imperfective auxiliary meaning ‘kept doing’ or ‘would do’ (habitual past). Here it governs the following noun-phrase کردار یاد: کردار (‘deed, action’) with یاد (‘remembrance’), together meaning ‘recalled the deeds’ or ‘made remembrance of the deed’. The verb is placed before its object phrase in the compact poetic line.
segments.s727-g83: ز (poetic ‘from/because of’) + گفتار او (‘his speech’) form the causal phrase. گردیه گشت سست is a small clause meaning ‘the gathered/assembled (force or resolving thing) became weak’: گشت is the intransitive verb ‘became/turned’, and سست is the resulting adjective ‘weak/lax’. The construction shows cause (ز گفتار او) followed by result (گردیه گشت سست).
segments.s727-g84: شداندیشه‌ها is the poetic compression of شد + اندیشه‌ها (‘thoughts became’): شد is the perfective/inchoative ‘became’ and اندیشه‌ها is its subject. بر دلش means ‘upon his heart’ (بر + دل + possessive suffix ش = ‘his’). The final بر درست is an adverbial phrase ‘firmly/properly’ (بر + درست), modifying the verb phrase: literally ‘thoughts became upon his heart firmly.’
segments.s727-g85: ببودند is an archaic/poetic variant of بودند (‘they were’); یکسر is an adverb ‘entirely/altogether’. به نزدیک اوی = ‘near him’ (به + نزدیک + اوی, with اوی = his). The clause therefore states that ‘they were wholly near him.’ Note the older/bound spelling (ببودند) common in epic diction.
segments.s727-g86: درخشان شد is ‘became shining/radiant’ (درخشان adjective, شد ‘became’). آن رای تاریک اوی = ‘that dark counsel/plan of his’: آن (that/the) + رای (here ‘plan/counsel’) + تاریک (dark) + اوی (‘his’). The noun رای takes the adjective تاریک; the whole becomes the subject of درخشان شد in inverted poetic order.
segments.s727-g87: یلان سینه را گفت is an idiomatic poetic phrase: یلان (‘champions/heroes’) + سینه را گفت literally ‘said to the chest’, i.e. ‘said inwardly/thought’ or ‘addressed their hearts’. کاین (‘that this’) introduces the reported content, زن (‘woman’) is the subject, and بشوی is a subjunctive/optative form of شد#شو here functioning as ‘will be / will do’ in the sense ‘will act’ or simply ‘will (be)’. The clause frames the inner question about the woman.
segments.s727-g88: چه گوید بجوید بدین آب روی is the reported question: چه (‘what/how’) + گوید (‘will say’ — here a verb meaning ‘say/will say’). بجوید is present/subjunctive ‘seeks/will seek’; بدین is the prepositional ‘with/for this’ (written here as ب + دین in the word list) and آب روی is an idiom ‘water-face’ = a poetic way to say ‘fair/beautiful face’. The whole means roughly ‘What will she say? Will she seek (him) for this fair face?’ Note some tokens were split (ب + دین) in the word list; they form the single prepositional particle بدین in the line.
segments.s727-g65: نخستین is an adverb/adjective meaning “first.” ز is the preposition “from” (poetic order) and takes تن “body” as its object: “from the body.” دست و پایش is a coordinated noun phrase “his hands and feet” (ش is the possessive clitic on پای). برید is a past tense verb “cut/severed”; the agent is not expressed (passive or implicit). Word order places the temporal adverb first, then the prepositional phrase, then the affected limbs, then the verb.
segments.s727-g66: بران سان (lit. “in that way/so”) is a fixed poetic expression meaning “in that manner” or “thus.” که introduces a relative clause “which/that.” از گوهر او literally “of/from his essence” here means “by/of his nature/character.” سزید is the past of سزا used impersonally/auxiliary-like: “it was fitting/deserved” — so the full clause means “in the manner that was fitting to his nature.”
segments.s727-g67: شما را places “you” as the (stressed/definite) object/topic. بدو is a prepositional/adverbial phrase “to him/in him.” چیست (here written ست as the copula) with چی (“what”) and اکنون (“now”) asks “what is your hope now?” — idiomatically: “What hope do you have in him now?” امید is the noun “hope.” Word order is topical (شما را) then indirect object (بدو) then predicate.
segments.s727-g68: کجا is the interrogative/locative “where.” همچو هنگام is a temporal phrase “at such a time/at a moment like this.” با is the preposition “with,” and دست و بید is a coordinated phrase “hand and willow.” The clause thus asks rhetorically where you will be/what you will do at such a moment, followed by the instruments/means “with hand and willow.”
segments.s727-g69: ابا is an archaic verb/noun meaning reluctance/refusal; here it introduces the idea that the doer will treat (or be unwilling/harsh toward) your fellows. همگنانتان is “your companions/peers” (plural + your suffix). بتر is comparative “worse,” زان “than that/from that,” and کند is the present/future-form verb “does/will do.” Together: “he will do worse to your companions than that.”
segments.s727-g70: به شهر اندرون is a locative phrase meaning “into/within the inner part of the city.” گوشت is “flesh/meat” and ارزان is “cheap” used adverbially, and کند again is “he does/makes” — here “he will sell the flesh cheaply” (i.e. offers it cheap in the inner city). The verb کند is used descriptively of the agent’s action.
segments.s727-g71: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” از دور is an adverbial “from afar/at a distance.” بیند is 3rd person present/historic-present of دیدن “sees.” یلان is the object “heroes,” and سینه را (with the object marker را) picks out the chest/heart as the affected part of the ensuing action. The clause sets the temporal scene: “When he sees the heroes from afar…”
segments.s727-g72: بر آشوبد is a verb phrase from آشوبیدن with the prefix بر (“become agitated/uplifted”); it means “his breast is stirred” or “he is thrown into turmoil.” و links the clauses. نو کند is “makes anew” (نو = new; کند = does/makes), and کینه را is the object “the hatred/grudge” (with را). Together: “he is stirred and renews the hatred.”
segments.s727-g25: سپاه (subject) + پراگنده کرد + انجمن (object) is a compact poetic order: literally 'army caused scattered assembly'. پراگنده is used predicatively/participial ('scattered') and کرد here has a causative force ('caused/made'), with the object انجمن expressed without the usual را. This is an S–(predicate)–O ordering typical of epic diction.
segments.s727-g26: همی‌تاخت is an archaic/imperfective verbal form with the continuous prefix همی- ('was charging' / 'kept charging'); the subject is implied (the army/soldiers). تا here marks the endpoint/extent ('as far as' / 'to') governing the following noun phrase بیشه نارون ('grove of elms'). Word order: verb + directional phrase is stylistically frequent in Ferdowsi.
segments.s727-g27: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. نزدیکی کوه آمل is a genitive/possession cluster 'the vicinity (nearness) of the mountain of Amol', and رسید is the simple past 'reached/arrived'. Together they form a temporal subordinate clause 'When (they) reached the vicinity of Mount Amol'.
segments.s727-g28: سپه را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker ('the army' as definite object). بد‌ان here is an archaic contraction (ب + دان) functioning like 'to/into that' (i.e. 'into that'). اندر کشید is a verbal phrase 'drew/dragged into' with کشید the past of کشیدن ('drew'); the subject is omitted (the commander or agent from context). Note some of the segment pieces are lexicalized poetically (ب + دان) rather than modern separate preposition + demonstrative.
segments.s727-g29: همی‌برد is an iterative/continuous verbal form ('kept carrying/sending'); بردن here has a causative/active sense 'send, carry out'. بر هر سوی is a prepositional phrase 'toward every side/direction'. تاختن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'charging' and functions as the complement describing what was carried out in every direction.
segments.s727-g30: بدان is again the archaic b + دان construction meaning 'in/at that' or 'to that'; تاختن is the verbal noun 'the charging'. بود is the past copula 'was'. کین (vengeance, hatred) modifies the following word, and آختن here is a verbal/poetic form meaning an act done in hatred — the whole phrase means 'that charging was an act borne of vengeance.' (Some small lexical pieces are archaic/poetic and appear contracted.)
segments.s727-g31: به هر سو که ... is an idiomatic construction 'whichever way / wherever'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'where'. بیکار مردم بدند uses بیکار 'idle' plus مردم 'people' as subject; بدند is an archaic/past-subjunctive or modal past form (here rendered 'were / would be'), giving a general conditional sense 'wherever people were idle'.
segments.s727-g32: به نانی is a short prepositional phrase 'for a morsel of bread' (به + نانی). همی adds a limiting/emphatic sense 'just/merely'. بنده او شدند is the predicate 'they became his servants' with او referring to the commander/agent; شدند is the past plural 'became'. The clause expresses that out of hunger/for a little bread people submitted as servants.
segments.s727-g33: This is an interrogative locative phrase: 'به جایی کجا' (= 'to what place / where') is an emphatic, somewhat poetic doubling of location words. Word order places the locative phrase before the subject 'لشکر شاه' (the king's army). The verb 'بود' is past tense 'was' (here used to ask where the army was/ had gone).
segments.s727-g34: The particle 'که' introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('for/that'). 'گستهم' is the proper name Gostahem (here treated as the subject). 'زان لشکر آگاه بود' is a simple past clause 'was aware of that army' with 'آگاه' as predicate adjective and 'بود' as the past copula. Word order is subject → object/phrase → predicate, typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s727-g35: 'همی' is an iterative/continuous marker used in epic Persian (here giving a repeated or forceful action). The preposition 'بر' governs 'سرانشان' ('upon their chiefs'). 'فرود آمدی' is the compound verb 'فرود آمدن' ('to descend/come down upon'); the past form 'آمدی' appears with a poetic/archaic ending and functions as a past-tense narrative verb (third-person in context).
segments.s727-g36: Here 'سپه' is the subject 'the host/army'. 'رایکایک' is an adverbial phrase 'one by one' (each in turn). 'بهم' means 'to one another' / 'at one another' and links to the action. 'برزدی' is an archaic/poetic past verb form (rare in modern Persian) meaning something like 'struck/overthrew'—the verb ends the clause and takes the preceding elements as its arguments.
segments.s727-g37: 'وزان پس' is a narrative connector 'and/from that then' ('after that'). 'چو' introduces a temporal/simile clause meaning 'when/as'. 'گردوی' is a simile-word ('walnut') used here as the complement of 'چو' ('when/as it became like a walnut'); 'شد' is the past 'became'. 'نزد شاه' is a locative phrase 'in the presence of / before the king'. Grammatically this is a time/setting clause that prepares the speech that follows.
segments.s727-g38: 'بگفت' is the past-tense narrative verb 'he said'. 'آن' is a demonstrative ('there/that') introducing the reported content. 'کجا' is the interrogative 'where' and governs the remainder: 'خواهرش با سپاه' = 'his sister with the army' (preposition 'با' follows the noun phrase). Word order in reported questions can be compact in older Persian: verb → discourse marker → interrogative → noun phrase.
segments.s727-g39: The cluster 'ب' + 'دان' here forms 'بدان' (imperative/attention-getting 'know this' or 'be aware'). It introduces a reported or emphatic clause. 'مرزبانان خاقان' is the genitive noun phrase 'the khagan's marzbans (frontier governors)'. 'چه کرد' is the question/relative 'what did (they) do' — 'چه' (what) + past verb 'کرد'.
segments.s727-g40: 'که' again introduces the explanatory subordinate clause. 'در مرو' is the locative 'in Merv'. 'زیشان' ('ز ایشان') means 'from them' / 'of them' and links back to the marzbans/people just mentioned. 'برآورد گرد' is a compound/verbal sequence: 'برآورد' (to bring/produce/raise — here used in a causative/performative sense) plus 'گرد' as an auxiliary/poetic element; together they form a past action 'brought about / raised' in a classical stylistic construction.
segments.s727-g49: چو is a subordinating conjunction meaning "when." آگاه شد is a past-clause: آگاه (aware) + شد (become) = "became aware." گردیه is the subject (a proper name here) and رفت پیش is the verb phrase: رفت (went) + پیش ("before/in the presence of"); note the verb follows the subject as in Persian.
segments.s727-g50: This is a prepositional/participant phrase describing where he went: از آموی here combines with پیش in the previous segment to indicate "in the presence of / before his uncle;" آموی = paternal uncle. با introduces accompaniment "with," نامدران is a plural noun "the famed/renowned ones," and خوی + ش forms خویش (the reflexive/possessive "his own"); the possessive is split across the two items as given.
segments.s727-g51: چو = "when." گستهم is a proper name (here the watcher/actor) despite its verbal look. دید is simple past "saw." آن سپه را is a definite object (آن = "that," سپه = "army," را = object marker). ز راه is a prepositional phrase "from/along the road," specifying where the army was seen.
segments.s727-g52: بر انگیخت is a past, transitive/causative-form verb here meaning "he urged/roused (into motion)"; the prefixed بر gives the sense of "upon/into action." اسپ is the direct object "steed/horse." از میان سپاه is a locative phrase "out of the midst of the army," where از میان = "out of/from the middle of."
segments.s727-g53: بیامد is the past tense "he came." بر before گردیه functions as a preposition meaning "to/toward" here ("came to Gurdiyah"). پر ز درد is an adjectival phrase "full of pain" (پر = "full (of)", ز = "of/from", درد = "pain").
segments.s727-g54: فراوان intensifies the clause: adverbially "greatly/abundantly." ز بهرام here marks the source/agent "from/by Bahram." تیمار خورد is an idiomatic verbal phrase: تیمار = "care/treatment," and خورد here functions in older/poetic Persian to mean "received/underwent" (i.e. "received care"); this is an idiomatic, somewhat archaic collocation.
segments.s727-g55: همان = "that same" (pointing back). درد = "pain/affliction." بندوی او را combines بندوی (bondman/servant) + او (his) with the object marker را attached after او: syntactically this reads as "he told his bondman" (او را = "him" marked as object). رابگفت is an archaic past "told/related it" (را + بگفت).
segments.s727-g56: همی is an adverb meaning "still/continually." به آستین = "onto his sleeve" (به = to/onto, آستین = sleeve). خون مژگان is a poetic image "the blood of the eyelashes" = tears; برفت is a prefixed past form meaning "went onto/fell upon" (بر‑ + رَفتن), describing the tears running onto the sleeve—an archaic/poetic motion verb here.
segments.s727-g73: که introduces this clause as a causal/explanatory conjunction ('because'). The core proposition is "سالار بودی تو": بودی is the past second-person form 'you were', with the pronoun تو placed after the verb (poetic inversion). بهرام را uses the object/topic marker را to highlight/mark Bahram as the person related to 'سالار' (i.e., 'commander of Bahram').
segments.s727-g74: This is the result clause. ازو ('from him') is the source; یافتی is past 'you obtained' (second‑person past). در جهان is a locative phrase 'in the world', and کام را ('the desire/satisfaction' + را) is the definite object, with را marking the object/emphasis. The clause reads literally 'from him you obtained in the world the satisfaction.'
segments.s727-g75: This clause begins with the source 'ازو' ('from him') and the universal relative 'هرکه' ('whoever'). داندش is a fused form 'knows him' (داند + object clitic ش). پرهیز is a noun 'caution/avoidance' used elliptically as an injunction ('beware'); به is the preposition 'toward/to', introducing the target of that caution.
segments.s727-g76: The phrase gives the concrete precaution: 'گلوی ... را خنجر تیز به' — a sharp dagger to (or at) the throat. گلوی is 'throat'; را marks the definite noun phrase; خنجر تیز is a noun + adjective 'sharp dagger'. The final به is the preposition 'to/at', completing the purpose/direction expressed earlier (beware by placing a dagger to the throat). Note the poetic word order (object phrases fronted/ordered for meter).
segments.s727-g77: گر‌ای combines the conditional particle گر ('if') with a vocative 'ای' addressing the addressees; دون is a vocative adjective 'base/ignoble ones'. که introduces the subordinate clause and باشید is the second‑person plural present subjunctive/optative meaning 'you be/will be' used here in an 'if' clause. با من بهم means 'with me, together' (بهم = together).
segments.s727-g78: This gives the instruction for participation in the council. ز نیم literally 'from half' or 'by half'; اندرین رای = 'in this counsel/plan' (اندرین = 'in these / in this'), رای is used as 'counsel/plan'. بر بیش و کم means 'for more and for less' — i.e. split the decision between 'more' and 'less'. Word order is arranged for poetic rhythm (preposing ز نیم).
segments.s727-g79: پذیرفت is the past tense 'accepted' (third‑person or generic past). ازو 'from him' indicates source of the counsel. هر که بشنید is a relative clause 'whoever heard (it)' — بشنید is past 'heard'. پند is 'advice/counsel' and functions as the object accepted. The clause is a simple past factual statement.
segments.s727-g80: همی‌جست is an imperfective/continuous past 'kept seeking' or 'sought (repeatedly)'. هر کس 'every person' is the subject. ز راه 'from the way' (i.e., 'on the road' or 'in the course') and گزند is the object 'harm/damage'; together they mean 'each person sought to avoid harm on the road' (literally 'sought, each person, protection from harm from the way').
segments.s727-g89: چنین داد پاسخ is an inverted order meaning “gave such an answer” (literally “thus gave answer”). داد is the past tense verb “gave.” که introduces the reported/purpose clause that follows. تا here expresses purpose/result (“so that / in order that”) rather than simple temporal “until.” گویمش is گویم + enclitic object -ش; گویم is 1st-person present/subjunctive ‘(that) I say / may I say’ and -ش = “it/her/him” (the thing or person to be told).
segments.s727-g90: به گفتار بسیار is an adverbial phrase “with / by means of many words” (به + noun used instrumentally). گفتار = “speech/words,” بسیار here means “many” (i.e. many words). دل جویمش is a light-verb construction: دل = “heart” (object), جویمش = جویم + -ش = “I seek / I will seek it/her”; together “I will seek her heart” i.e. “I will woo/win her heart.” جویم is present/subjunctive/optative sense often used for intended action.
segments.s727-g91: یلان is the plural subject “the heroes.” سینه با گردیه is an adverbial descriptive phrase modifying how they spoke: سینه = “chest/bosom,” با + گردیه = “with (a) turned/heaving (chest)” (poetic description). گفت is the past tense verb “said,” and زن at the end is a vocative or direct address “O woman.”
segments.s727-g92: به گیتی = a locative phrase “in the world” (به used idiomatically with گیتی). تو را دیده‌ام is a fronted object + perfect construction: تو را (object-marked) + دیده‌ام “I have seen you.” رای زن is an appositive/vocative tag: رای = “counsel/wise opinion” used here as an epithet “of counsel,” and زن = “woman,” so together addressing her as “O woman of counsel” or “wise woman.”
segments.s727-g93: ز is the poetic preposition “from/of.” خاقان is the proper title “Khaghan.” کرانه گزیدی is a verb phrase “you chose the frontier/border” (کرانه = border/edge, گزیدی = 2nd-person past of گزیدن “to choose”). سزید here is an archaic/past-form meaning “it was fitting / you deserved” (senses include ‘to deserve’ / ‘befit’); it functions as an evaluative predicate referring back to the action.
segments.s727-g94: که introduces the subordinate clause giving a reason/explanation (“for/that”). رای here = “counsel/plan.” تو is the 2nd-person pronoun “you.” آزادگان is the plural “the free (men, nobles).” را is the object marker attached to آزادگان. گزید is the past tense “chose” (the verb گزیدن), so the clause means “for your counsel chose the freemen.”
segments.s727-g95: چه گویی is a rhetorical question “what do you say?” (چه = what/how; گویی = 2nd-person present ‘you say’). ز introduces the topic “about/regarding.” گستهم here is the proper name Gostaham (a hero); the provided lexical sense (‘I wandered’) is not the intended sense in this line (proper-name usage). یل = “hero/brave man.” خال شاه is an epithet: خال = “mark/mole” used figuratively and شاه = “king,” together a descriptive epithet ‘marked/by the king’ or ‘the king’s mark.’
segments.s727-g96: توانگر سپهبد یلی با سپاه is a stacked nominal phrase describing the person: توانگر = “wealthy,” سپهبد = the title “spahbed” (a high military rank), یلی = “valiant/mighty,” با سپاه = “with an army.” The sequence lists rank/qualities and the instrument/force (سپاه) he commands.
segments.s727-g57: This clause has a normal SOV/Persian object-marking order: یلان (subject) + سینه را (object marked by را) + دید (past verb). The conjunction و then begins a coordinated second clause; ایزد گشسپ is a nominative phrase (honorific + name) that will be the subject of that following clause.
segments.s727-g58: فرود آمد is a compound past action (verb stem + آمد) meaning ‘descended / came down.’ از دور is an ablative phrase ‘from afar,’ and the participial/adjunct گریان modifies the subject (Goshasp) — زاسپ (= ز اسب) says he left his horse (dismounted). Word order is poetic but functions as ‘[Subject] descended from afar, weeping, off his horse.’
segments.s727-g59: بگفت is a past reporting verb ‘he said.’ آنک (= that/which) introduces the content clause; بندوی را is the definite object ('the bondman' marked by را) and شهریار is the agent named as the one who acted. The clause means ‘he said that the king [did X] to the bondman.’
segments.s727-g60: تبه کرد is a verb phrase where تبه (mischief/ruin) + کرد (‘did/caused’) means ‘did wrong’ or ‘ruined.’ The coordinating و links the next result clause: بد شد مرا روزگار literally ‘my days/fate became bad,’ where مرا is the object (here ‘to me’/‘my’) and روزگار is the subject/semantic theme ‘fate/fortune.’
segments.s727-g61: تو گفتی is an address to ‘you’ plus a past speech verb ‘you said.’ نه ... زاده بود is a negated past copular clause: نه از خواهرش زاده بود = ‘he was not born of his sister.’ Note خواهرش is ‘his sister’ (possessive suffix ش) and زاده بود is the past passive/nominal 'was born / was of the lineage.'
segments.s727-g62: The second negated clause uses نه ... بود again: نه از بهر او تن به خون داده بود = ‘nor had he given his body to blood for him.’ Here از بهر او = ‘for him,’ تن = ‘body,’ خون داده بود is a perfect/pluperfect construction meaning ‘had shed blood’ or ‘had bled / been wounded (for him).’
segments.s727-g63: This clause is idiomatic: به تارک مر او را روا داشتی. به + تارک مر = ‘to my head/crown’ (تارک here is a poetic word for the crown or top of the head) and او را روا داشتی literally ‘you considered him permissible/fit’ — روا داشتی (râva + past auxiliary) = ‘you deemed acceptable/fit.’ The past-tense داشتـی marks the second-person past habitual/perfect.
segments.s727-g64: روان پیش خاکش فدا داشتی is a compact devotional image: روان = ‘(your) soul/life,’ پیش خاکش = ‘before his dust’ (i.e. in his presence or to honor him), فدا داشتی = ‘you had sacrificed / would have sacrificed.’ Again داشتی is the past form meaning ‘you had/you would have.’ The two clauses together emphasize that the addressee elevated and would even sacrifice himself for that person.
segments.s727-g41: وزان is a compound 'و' + 'زان' = 'and from that' (a connective + ablative). روی گستهم is a poetic noun phrase: گستهم is the past-1sg verbal form 'I wandered' used here as a nominal/participial stem ('my wandering' or 'my roaming'), governed by روی (an old relational noun/covering sense 'regarding/on the matter of'); بشنید is simple past 3rd-person 'he heard' and نیز 'also/likewise' links this clause to the previous information.
segments.s727-g42: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that...'. بهرام یل is the name + appositional epithet 'Bahram the hero'; را here functions as a topical/definite marker attached to یل (a poetic/emphatic use rather than a strict accusative). پر آمد is a past-tense verbal phrase literally 'came/full came' (here rendered 'arrived/appeared' with the sense 'was filled/arrived'), and قفیز is a rare/obscure noun left as a lexical item in apposition/complement; the clause therefore reports that Bahram (the hero) 'came' in relation to that qafiz (the exact nuance is lexical/poetic rather than syntactic).
segments.s727-g43: همان is a demonstrative/adjective 'that same' or 'the very (one)'. گردیه is a poetic/past form (third person) meaning 'he turned/rode off / set out' (an archaic past of گردیدن). با سپاه بزرگ is a prepositional phrase 'with a great army' (با + noun + adjective), giving the accompaniment of the action.
segments.s727-g44: برفت is here an older/perhaps dialectal past-form meaning 'he went off / departed' (note: the lemma list given did not include this sense, so the provided senses did not match). از بر is an ablative or 'from the presence/of' construction (از = 'from', بر = 'before/presence/at'), and نامدار سترگ is a nominal phrase 'the renowned/highly eminent one' (نامدار = 'famous', سترگ an archaic epithet meaning 'great/lofty'); the whole yields 'he departed from the presence of the famed great (one)'.
segments.s727-g45: پس is a connective 'then/thereupon'. او is the 3rd-person pronoun 'he'. سپاهی is a noun used either as 'an army/troops' or as an appositive 'a warrior/one of the host' depending on context. بیامد is past 'came/arrived' (here part of the verbal periphrasis describing mustering or movement), and بکین is a poetic infinitival/finite form (archaic variant of بکن or a purpose/result-form) meaning 'to do' or 'to effect' (here implying 'to do battle' or 'to take action'); the clause thus says he mobilized/appeared with troops to carry out an action.
segments.s727-g46: چه کرد او is the standard past interrogative 'what did he do?': چه (what) + کرد (did) + او (he). بدان here is an archaic two-part prepositional sequence written as ب + دان in the segmentation: ب = 'to/against/toward' and دان in this passage should be read as part of that locution (the provided senses for دان do not capture this archaic function, so sense is marked -1); نامداران چین is a genitive phrase 'the famed/illustrious men of China' (نامداران = 'renowned ones', چین = 'China' or 'the region of China'). The whole asks what he did to those famous men of China.
segments.s727-g47: پذیره شدن is a deverbal nominal phrase 'the reception / being received' (پذیره as noun 'reception' + شدن as the deverbal marker), and را marks that phrase as the (definite) object/topic. سپه is 'the army' (archaic/formal word for 'army') and برنشاند is a causative/past verb 'he set/place(d)/deployed' (برنشاند = caused to be set in place). Grammatically, he 'set the army (in order) for the reception' (i.e. deployed troops to receive/meet someone).
segments.s727-g48: ازان جایگه is an ablative phrase 'from that place' (ازان = 'from that', جایگه = 'place/position'). نیز 'also' links this action to what preceded. لشکر is another word for 'army/host', and براند (here a past form of راندن/رانْدَن) means 'drove away' or 'drove on' — together: 'from that spot he likewise drove the host away.' Word order shows the object (لشکر) before the verb, typical SOV in classical/poetic Persian.

sentences.s727-s49: He said to him, “You are one who came from Iran; our stock was not ruined by him.”
sentences.s727-s50: The heroes gave breadth to his chest; he was called a valiant man of fortunate lineage.
sentences.s727-s51: He held him like a fresh apple, which you have not seen fall from on high.
sentences.s727-s52: A warrior who had been in Khosrow’s presence; then the old age became new.
sentences.s727-s5: He said that this was Parviz's command — that the shah was young and bloodthirsty.
sentences.s727-s6: When Gostaham heard, he drove off the army; he called back the scattered host.
sentences.s727-s7: Thus he proceeded until he reached the city of the great; from Sari and Amol he came to Gorgan.
sentences.s727-s8: He heard that the shah of Iran had become harsh; in drunkenness he had killed his brother.
sentences.s727-s53: Whenever you saw the army routed, you lifted your bow up toward the moon.
sentences.s727-s1: After that, someone went toward Khorasan and gave him much counsel.
sentences.s727-s2: He said to him, 'Do not move your tongue toward anyone; go from here to the marzban (the frontier governor).'
sentences.s727-s3: Tell Gostaham: 'Do not do anything of this kind; when you read this letter of mine, come.'
sentences.s727-s4: When the envoy reached Khorasan, he came to the court and encountered a mild-mannered man.
sentences.s727-s9: When he heard it, he bit his hand; he sprang down from the back of his war-horse.
sentences.s727-s10: He rent his whole garment at the side, and, roaring, threw dust upon his head.
sentences.s727-s11: He understood where the sovereign king would bring ruin out of vengeance for his father.
sentences.s727-s12: Roaring, he withdrew from that place; you said that he returned like the wind.
sentences.s727-s41: With a sharp tongue and gathered words he opened his speech, remembering the deeds of Bahram.
sentences.s727-s42: Because of his words the gathered resolve grew weak; thoughts became firmly fixed upon his heart.
sentences.s727-s43: They were all entirely near him; that dark counsel of his became radiant.
sentences.s727-s44: The champions said to their breasts, ‘What will this woman say? Will she seek (him) for that fair face?’
sentences.s727-s33: First his hands and feet were cut from his body, in the manner that befitted his nature.
sentences.s727-s34: What hope do you now have in him? Where, at a moment like this, (are you) with hand and willow?
sentences.s727-s35: He will do worse to your companions than that; in the inner city he will sell the flesh cheaply.
sentences.s727-s36: When he sees the heroes from afar, he stirs his breast and renews his hatred.
sentences.s727-s13: The army scattered the assembly and charged together as far as the grove of elms.
sentences.s727-s14: When they reached the vicinity of Mount Amol, he drew the army into that grove.
sentences.s727-s15: He kept sending charges in every direction; that charging was an act of vengeance.
sentences.s727-s16: Wherever the people were idle, they would become his servants for a morsel of bread.
sentences.s727-s17: Where had the king’s army gone? Gostahem, however, was aware of that army.
sentences.s727-s18: He swooped down upon their chiefs; the host, one by one, struck (them) in turn.
sentences.s727-s19: Then, after that, when he was brought before the king, he said there, 'Where is his sister with the army?'
sentences.s727-s20: Know, then, what the khagan's marzbans did: that in Merv they raised (troops) from them.
sentences.s727-s25: When Gurdiyah became aware, he went forward into the presence of his uncle with his famed men.
sentences.s727-s26: When Gostaham saw that army coming along the road, he spurred his steed out from among the host.
sentences.s727-s27: He came to Gurdiyah, full of pain; he received abundant care from Bahram.
sentences.s727-s28: He told that same affliction to his bondman; continually the blood of his eyelashes ran onto his sleeve.
sentences.s727-s37: Because you were Bahram's commander, you obtained from him worldly satisfaction.
sentences.s727-s38: Whoever knows him should beware of him; (keep) a sharp dagger at his throat.
sentences.s727-s39: If you base (ignoble) ones will join me, then in this counsel split the vote — half for more and half for less.
sentences.s727-s40: Whoever heard his counsel accepted it; every man sought to avoid harm on the road.
sentences.s727-s45: He answered thus: "So that I may tell it at length, with many words I will seek (win) her heart."
sentences.s727-s46: The heroes, with heaving chests, said to the woman: "I have seen your like in the world, O woman of counsel."
sentences.s727-s47: You chose the Khaghan's border — it was fitting — for your counsel chose the free (men).
sentences.s727-s48: What do you say of Gostaham, the king‑marked hero — a wealthy spahbed, a mighty warrior with an army?
sentences.s727-s29: The heroes saw the chest, and Goshasp the illustrious came down from afar, dismounting from his horse, weeping.
sentences.s727-s30: He said that the king had wronged the bondman, and my fortunes have turned bad.
sentences.s727-s31: You said he was not born of his sister, nor had he shed his body in blood for him.
sentences.s727-s32: You had made him worthy of my crown; you had offered your soul as a sacrifice before his dust.
sentences.s727-s21: And from that he likewise heard — that Bahram the hero had come (arrived) with the obscure/rare thing called qafiz.
sentences.s727-s22: That very Gurdiyah, with a great army, set out — and departed from the presence of the renowned one.
sentences.s727-s23: Then he (or they) mustered troops for action; what did he do to those famed men of China?
sentences.s727-s24: He stationed the army for reception; from that place likewise he drove the host away.
